change_summary: Queue 238: decider c10 patches — finding formula corrected to 3-factor form (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF), misattributed BOU clause removed, P3 scope declaration restructured. This c11 re-attack verifies the patches landed and looks for residual scope-creep and new holes.
our_argument_summary: WIN-068 claims the Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble (1991–2016) confirms the dome's 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' via the toroidal cavity induction gradient. We rate it 'Std Model Explains': the Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq mechanism predicts the ensemble signal (Y up, X/Z/F down, 30–50° latitude peak, ~180-min timescale) without any dome parameter; the ensemble attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction (FSF is only relevant to the WIN-010 2026 forward projection); and the observation is triple-counted with WIN-010 and WIN-025 at different aggregation levels. We credit FSF = H(r)/r as a genuine dome-geometric derivation (retraction of prior 'empirical fitting' framing) but scope that concession to WIN-010.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome's eclipse scaling equation lives in 03-predictions.txt lines 21–23: 'delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF'. FSF is listed as derived from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry; baselines are given as 'BOU 2017 (-10.9 nT)' and 'W004 empirical (-22.24 nT)'. WIN-068 itself is not represented in the raw V50.6 raw-text/02-wins.txt corpus (which only extends to WIN-039); WIN-068 is a V51 addition citing Kim & Chang (2018) as a retrospective ensemble confirmation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No live comparison exists in the dome's automation for WIN-068. The equation drives the 2026 forward projection (WIN-010 scope), not the 1991–2016 Kim & Chang ensemble counted here.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 (post-hoc literature citation)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None monitored. Raw text V50.6 has no entry for a 39-eclipse ensemble WIN; the claim was incorporated by the dome in V51.0 early 2026 — eight years after Kim & Chang published.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None material at c11. The c10 finding fix to 3-factor 'delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF' now matches 03-predictions.txt line 21 verbatim. See new moderate hole H1 below for a residual scope leak inside detail_verdict_text ¶5.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py is present in the current raw-text/ snapshot. The raw V50.6 text (which is the most recent scrape) has no WIN-068 entry at all — the dome added this claim in V51.0 citing Kim & Chang (2018) retrospectively. No live API fetch or pred==obs comparison for the 39-eclipse ensemble exists in dome automation; the 2026-forward scaling equation in 03-predictions.txt targets WIN-010, not this ensemble.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) Sq-diurnal framework plus the Yamazaki & Maute (2021) conductivity update, together with Kim & Chang's own interpretation, derive the 39-eclipse ensemble pattern from independently-sourced ionospheric parameters (E-region electron density, conductivity tensors from ionosondes/GPS/TEC). The dome relabels the same observed signal as 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' without deriving the component pattern, latitude dependence, or timescale from dome geometry.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) covers 1991–2016 INTERMAGNET data, published in Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054. The dome first counts this as a WIN in V51.0 (early 2026), an ~8-year gap. No pre-registration of the expected ensemble result in a venue independent of the dome author has been located.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The ensemble contains no dome-derived numerical prediction. FSF = H(r)/r is a dome-geometric derivation (retracted our 'empirical fitting' framing; Pearson r = 0.9999 against published FSF), but FSF is only invoked for the 2026 forward projection (WIN-010). For the 1991–2016 ensemble the absolute magnitudes come directly from Kim & Chang's published dataset.

holes_found.description: Scope leak in detail_verdict_text ¶5 (the 'Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling…' paragraph). The immediately preceding paragraphs correctly scope the WIN: 'The 39-eclipse ensemble evaluated here does not invoke FSF at all' (detail_evidence ¶3) and 'FSF does not enter the 1991–2016 data at all — it is only invoked for the 2026 forward projection' (detail_verdict_text ¶2). Then ¶5 spends a whole paragraph attacking FSF via the Coordinate Collapse. For a WIN whose own verdict says FSF is a spectator, burning a paragraph inside the verdict on FSF coordinate contamination is the same scope violation the c10 patch was explicitly designed to remove ('misattributed BOU clause removed, P3 scope declaration restructured'). A dome defender will quote our own scope sentence and ask why we then re-attack FSF in the same verdict.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) delete ¶5 from WIN-068.detail_verdict_text and move its content into WIN-010's verdict with a one-sentence cross-reference from WIN-068 ('For an FSF-specific critique inheriting the Section 1.8 refractive contamination, see WIN-010'); or (b) rewrite ¶5 to explicitly lampshade the scope: 'We note this as inheritance-relevant to WIN-010 only — it does not affect WIN-068's ensemble verdict, which rests on Chapman Sq.' Option (a) is cleaner.

holes_found.description: detail_verdict_text ¶2 recapitulates the same 2-factor drop the c10 finding patch fixed: 'every other station's predicted delta_Z is a 2017 BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio.' That phrasing omits the coverage term from the dome's 3-factor equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, 03-predictions.txt line 21). A defender can pull the same string we just corrected in the finding and show we're still dropping coverage one paragraph later.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite as 'every other station's predicted delta_Z is the 2017 BOU magnitude scaled by the station's obscuration coverage and its FSF = H(r)/r geometric ratio' — matches the 3-factor form the c10 finding patch adopted.

holes_found.description: detail_verdict_text ¶6 subject ambiguity: 'WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble 1991–2016) all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism at three different aggregation levels.' The verb 'invoke' is subject-ambiguous — a naive reader could parse it as 'the dome invokes Chapman Sq,' which is false (the dome invokes cavity shading; we invoke Chapman Sq to explain the observation). A defender will misread this uncharitably and claim we're putting Sq in the dome's mouth.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite as 'WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble 1991–2016) all cite observations explained by the same ionospheric Sq mechanism at three different aggregation levels.' Keeps the triple-counting punch, removes the grammatical ambiguity about who invokes what.

holes_found.description: Finding wording: 'but the 1991–2016 Kim & Chang ensemble evaluated here attaches no dome-derived prediction' is over-broad. The dome DOES attach a dome-derived mechanism claim ('firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' via toroidal cavity). What it does not attach is a dome-derived NUMERICAL or QUANTITATIVE prediction. A pedantic defender can point to the mechanism sentence in the dome's claim and say 'you said we attach no dome-derived prediction, but the mechanism is a dome-derived prediction — strawman.'
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten to 'attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction' or 'attaches no dome-derived quantitative prediction'. One word; removes the defender's opening.

holes_found.description: Chapman (1933) mis-specialization. detail_evidence ¶3 and detail_verdict_text ¶1 both frame Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) as the mechanism that 'predicts the ensemble signal.' Chapman 1933 is 'On the diurnal variations of the earth's magnetic field' — the foundational paper on Sq as a continuous daytime current, not on eclipse perturbations specifically. Eclipse-Sq work postdates it (e.g., Price 1965; Rastogi 1962; Malin 1973; and later Yamazaki & Maute 2016/2021 for the modern treatment). We already cite Yamazaki & Maute 2021 as the conductivity framework, which is correct. A sharp defender can claim we're inflating Chapman's 1933 paper to cover eclipse ensemble prediction, which he did not actually do.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe as 'the Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework — foundational in Chapman (1933) and applied specifically to eclipses in subsequent work (Rastogi 1962, Malin 1973) with modern conductivity treatment by Yamazaki & Maute (2021)' — or more compact: 'The mechanism is the Sq current vortex described by Chapman (1933) and applied to eclipses in subsequent ionospheric work.' Keeps the claim strong without over-attributing.

holes_found.description: ISS-1441 still open: Chapman (1933) citation format ambiguous. detail_evidence ¶3 reads 'Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3)' — vol:page versus vol:issue is unresolved. Flagged in c10; not patched in c10 because the queue item scope was the finding/BOU-clause/P3 fixes.
holes_found.recommendation: Same as ISS-1441 — specify 'Chapman (1933), Terr. Mag. Atmos. Elec. 38(4):445–482' if that is the correct paper, or switch to a better-specified Chapman reference (1919 Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118, or Chapman & Bartels 1940 Geomagnetism). Overlaps with the new H5 above.

holes_found.description: ISS-1442 still open: Tier-1 Steelman admission sentence is verbatim-duplicated between detail_evidence ¶4 and detail_verdict_text ¶4. Not patched in c10 because the queue item scope was elsewhere. A style issue only, but a dome defender can point to the duplication as sloppy editing.
holes_found.recommendation: Keep the admission in detail_verdict_text ¶4 (higher rhetorical impact in the verdict) and replace the evidence-side occurrence with a brief cross-reference: 'See the verdict for the dome's own Tier-1 concession.'

holes_found.description: ISS-1443 still open: detail_evidence ¶2 reads 'the refraction fix leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate' — stale phrasing that predates the EXP-222/HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001 renaming of Section 1.8 to 'The Coordinate Collapse' (which is correctly named in the link anchor text in detail_verdict_text ¶5). The inline prose here still refers to 'the refraction fix,' which the reader may not be able to map to the current Section 1.8.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase as 'the n(r) refractive contamination leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate' or 'the Coordinate Collapse (Section 1.8) leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate' — consistent with the current Section 1.8 framing.

stronger_arguments.description: The single hardest-to-escape attack on WIN-068 is a volume argument: Chapman-Sq eclipse modeling makes station-level, component-level, latitude-dependent, and temporal-shape predictions from independently-sourced parameters (ionosonde/TEC/climatological electron density and conductivity), zero of which are fitted to the eclipse signal. The dome's matching of a station-to-station scaling ratio (FSF) is one number per station, normalized to another dome-produced number (BOU 2017), on a dataset not even used in this WIN. Count the independent degrees of freedom constrained: Chapman–Sq constrains O(100s) (each Y/X/Z/F/time-bin per station), dome FSF (not even used here) constrains 1 (the ratio shape). Adding one sentence to detail_verdict_text spelling out this asymmetry in degrees-of-freedom would make the non-discrimination verdict much harder to contest.
sources: Yamazaki & Maute 2021, Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences
Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054

stronger_arguments.description: Internal-consistency hit we still aren't pursuing: the dome attributes the eclipse magnetic signal to 'the pressurized toroidal cavity's induction gradient' — a cavity whose geometry is set by disc_radius and firmament_height, i.e., physical (not magnetic) geometry. But Kim & Chang's 30°–50° peak is specifically at MAGNETIC latitudes — the tilted dipole Sq-vortex signature. The dome has no published mechanism by which a dome-geometric (physical-latitude) cavity would produce a peak that tracks magnetic latitudes, which rotate with secular dipole motion relative to dome coordinates. Adding this as a one-sentence hit in detail_verdict_text ¶1 converts 'non-discriminating' into 'actively contradicted by latitude structure' — a stronger verdict than we currently claim.
sources: IGRF-13 / CHAOS-7 for dipole axis position

stronger_arguments.description: We never attack the triple-counting numerically. 'WIN-010 + WIN-025 + WIN-068 for one mechanism' would be more damning if we said: 'If triple-counting is allowed, the dome's WIN total should be re-denominated by independence-class — three eclipse WINs collapse to one, yielding a 2-WIN reduction to ~66 confirmed predictions. This is not pedantry: the standard-physics analog would be equivalent to counting 'Newtonian gravity' as one WIN for every apple-drop experiment.' A one-sentence quantification of the score deflation gives a reader a concrete takeaway.

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) is genuine, peer-reviewed, methodologically sound science. The 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble does show the claimed pattern (Y up, X/Z/F down, magnetic latitude 30°–50° peak, ~180-min timescale centered on maximum eclipse). The dome's separate FSF = H(r)/r station-scaling derivation is also real: solving r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) and normalizing to Boulder reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999, better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations. Our prior 'empirical fitting' framing of FSF was quantitatively wrong and has been retracted in the WIN-010/025/068 cluster.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: FSF is a WIN-010 ingredient, not a WIN-068 one: the 1991–2016 ensemble evaluated here does not invoke FSF at all, so the dome's one genuine derivation in this cluster is a spectator for this WIN. And even for WIN-010, the FSF scaling shape is not uniquely dome-geometric — any smooth monotonic height function combined with r·tan(θ) = H(r) produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same functional form. Matching observations to 0.1% with one specific H(r) parametrization is consistent with the dome but does not discriminate it from the family of alternative disc models. The Sq current-vortex framework, meanwhile, derives the component pattern, latitude dependence, and timescale from ionospheric parameters independently sourced (ionosondes, GPS/TEC) and fits nothing to the eclipse signal being predicted.

deeper_analysis:
The c10 patch did what it said on the tin: the finding formula is now 3-factor and matches 03-predictions.txt line 21 verbatim, the 'absolute magnitude borrows the 2017 BOU eclipse observation as its fitted baseline' misattribution has been removed from the finding, and the P3 scope declaration is now explicit ('The 39-eclipse ensemble evaluated here does not invoke FSF at all'). The residual problem is that the scope discipline the c10 patch achieved in paragraphs 1–3 of detail_evidence and paragraphs 1–2 of detail_verdict_text is partially undone in detail_verdict_text ¶5, which launches a Coordinate-Collapse attack on FSF inside a WIN whose own scope sentence says FSF is a spectator. A dome defender can surface that contradiction by quoting our own scope sentence against us. The fix is a one-paragraph delete-and-cross-reference, keeping the Coordinate-Collapse attack alive in WIN-010's verdict where it belongs. The minor holes below (H2 coverage-factor drop in ¶2 verdict, H3 'invoke' ambiguity, H4 finding overreach on 'no dome-derived prediction', H5 Chapman-1933 over-attribution) are individually cheap to patch and the three pre-existing open issues (ISS-1441/1442/1443) remain unresolved from c10.

Advocate-mode (dome defender) angle we should actively pre-empt: 'Our Steelman Evidence Ladder admits Tier-1 evidence is consistent with both models. We never claimed WIN-068 was discriminating on its own — it is a confirmed observation inside a cluster of evidence, one brick in a wall.' This is actually the strongest defender framing and our verdict text deflects it only indirectly. Consider a sentence in detail_verdict_text ¶4 acknowledging the brick-in-wall defense and countering it: 'Confirming a pre-existing 1933 mechanism with 2018 published data does not turn the observation into a brick in a dome-specific wall — it is, by the dome's own Tier-1 admission, a brick in the Sq wall too.'

Cross-WIN consistency check: the c4 review for WIN-010 (the WIN this ensemble implicitly rides on) should mirror the FSF/H(r)/r retraction and the coordinate-collapse-via-Section-1.8 critique that ¶5 of this verdict is trying to host. If WIN-010's detail_verdict_text already contains the coordinate-collapse critique, deleting ¶5 here is a clean scope fix. If WIN-010 does not yet carry it, the delete-and-cross-reference requires adding the paragraph to WIN-010 first — a separate EXP/issue, not a WIN-068 patch. Recommend the decider check WIN-010's current verdict text before popping this review.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018) — Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054. Title: 'Geomagnetic field variations observed by INTERMAGNET observatories during solar eclipses' (approx.). DOI exists in ADS/Elsevier. Volume/page plausible for 2018 issue 8.

Yamazaki & Maute (2021) — Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences. URL (frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fspas.2021.766327/full) is a live Frontiers article on Sq current conductivity — cross-verified shape of claim against known Yamazaki/Maute review work.
citations_unchecked: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) — cited vol/page ambiguous. Chapman 1933 title is 'On the diurnal variations of the earth's magnetic field.' Paper is foundational Sq, not eclipse-specific. Flagged under H5 and H6/ISS-1441.
Kim & Chang (2018) 180-minute window claim — verified shape but exact numerical window (180 min vs e.g. 150 min or 210 min) not independently confirmed. Low risk; claim is qualitatively standard.
